relatability_triggers: Lead type: Revolutionary revelation + historical context

headline.primary: A business miracle... just when we all need a business miracle
headline.subheadline: One company cut its staff in half, tripled sales, and pushed profits through the roof. See for yourself how powerful and simple I-Power is.
headline.tertiary_headline: In a time when nothing works, here's one thing that really does.

pattern_analysis.curiosity_mechanism: What is I-Power and how did it produce miraculous results?

schema_analysis.expectation_violated: Business breakthrough doesn't come from new strategies but from rediscovering American principle that fueled Japanese miracle

lead.full_text:
A LITTLE BOOK has started a quiet revolution in plants and offices all across the United States of America. The principles of scientific management, unquestioned for decades, are being challenged—and banished to the dustbin of history.

A new breed of managers is returning to the commonsense practice of the great days when we were out-producing and out-selling the rest of the world... the days before management by objectives... before short-term thinking, short-term incentives, short-term goals...

This book has become the bible of that business revolution. It will show you a whole other way to run a company... a department... a business career. I-Power is for people on top... and the bottom... and the many, many places in between.

Insiders call this system Continuous Improvement... I-POWER for short.
lead.opening_mechanism: Revolution announcement + nostalgia for American greatness
lead.curiosity_creation: What is this forgotten American principle that's revolutionizing business?
lead.proof_preview: One company cut staff in half, tripled sales, profits through roof

big_idea.statement: American business is being destroyed by short-term scientific management thinking. The solution isn't new—it's rediscovering I-Power (Continuous Improvement), the American-invented system that fueled Japan's miracle and can restore American competitive dominance.
big_idea.supporting_idea: I-Power was pioneered by American companies (Kodak 1890s, GE/Westinghouse/Met Life early 1900s, Bell Labs 1920s, US war machine 1940s) but forgotten after WWII when emphasis shifted from product excellence to marketing. Japan learned it from us and dominated. Now we must reclaim it.

mechanism.name: I-Power (Continuous Improvement)
mechanism.primary_mechanism: Instead of top-down management by objectives with short-term incentives, I-Power creates culture where every employee continuously improves products/processes, focusing on long-term product excellence over short-term results. This unleashes innovation, quality, and sustainable competitive advantage.

mechanism_reveal_architecture.proof_to_mechanism_ratio: Heavy on historical examples and results

promise.primary_promise: I-Power will change the way you think about every element of business and can help your company dominate competitors like it did for Japanese companies and early American pioneers
promise.promise_specificity: Change thinking about cost control, quality control, productivity, staff meetings, delegation, hiring, firing, labor relations, executive compensation, incentive programs, profit-sharing

believability_factors: Historical track record (Kodak 1890s, GE, Westinghouse, Bell Labs)
Author credentials (editor-in-chief Bottom Line/Personal, 2M+ copies sold)
Robert Half endorsement: 'Best business book I've ever read'

strongest_proof_elements.text: One company cut its staff in half, tripled sales, and pushed profits through the roof using I-Power

Great American corporations pioneered I-Power: Eastman Kodak 1890s, General Electric/Westinghouse/Met Life/Parke-Davis early 1900s, Bell Laboratories 1920s, US war machine 1940s. It fueled Japanese miracle—we taught them much more than Quality Circles.

Robert Half (Robert Half International): 'I-Power is the best business book I've ever read... and I've read them all.' Dr. Leonard Sayles (Center for Creative Leadership): 'Throw away all your fancy books on management theory and read I-POWER.'

proof_inventory.proof_density: Moderate—relies on historical examples and dramatic results

offer_components: Great American corporations that pioneered I-Power
Forgotten role in building US war machine 1940s
Why simple, powerful American idea got lost
Inside look at how it works (Kodak to Anheuser-Busch)
I-Power for small companies wanting to be big
I-Power for big companies wanting to stay big
urgency_elements: Bad times create urgency for business miracle

story_elements.primary_story_type: Historical rise, fall, and rediscovery narrative
story_beats: American golden age: out-producing and out-selling world
American companies pioneer I-Power (Kodak 1890s, GE early 1900s)
I-Power builds US war machine dominance 1940s
Post-WWII shift: American business abandons product excellence for marketing
I-Power forgotten in America, advocates overwhelmed
Japan learns I-Power from America, dominates with it
American companies suffer under scientific management
Quiet revolution: American managers rediscovering I-Power
One company: cuts staff in half, triples sales, profits through roof
Promise: You can reclaim American competitive dominance
story_elements.emotional_progression: Nostalgia (American greatness) → Frustration (we forgot it) → Shame (Japan learned it from us) → Anger (scientific management destroyed us) → Hope (quiet revolution happening) → Pride (reclaim American dominance)

metadata.author: Martin Edelston
metadata.target_audience: Business owners, managers, executives seeking competitive advantage and productivity breakthroughs

fssit_candidates.statement: The principles of scientific management, unquestioned for decades, are being challenged—and banished to the dustbin of history. A new breed of managers is returning to the commonsense practice of the great days when we were out-producing and out-selling the rest of the world... the days before management by objectives... before short-term thinking, short-term incentives, short-term goals.
fssit_candidates.past_explaining_power: Explains why modern American business feels broken—it abandoned the I-Power principles that made America great in favor of short-term scientific management

fssit_candidates.statement: How American I-Power fueled the 'Japanese miracle' (we taught them much, much more than Quality Circles)... Why such a simple, powerful American idea got lost in America. How its advocates were overwhelmed when postwar American business switched its emphasis from product excellence to marketing.
fssit_candidates.past_explaining_power: Explains the humiliating pattern where Japan dominated using OUR system—we forgot our own greatness

rsf_analysis.fssit_placement_pattern: FSSIT establishes American pride/shame tension, positions I-Power as reclaiming lost greatness

schema_analysis.expectation_violated: Business success doesn't require new management theories—it requires returning to forgotten American principles
schema_analysis.whitespace_territory: Continuous Improvement vs. scientific management short-termism

narrative_reorganization.domain_scope: Business management, competitive strategy, organizational culture
narrative_reorganization.self_blame_removal: Your business didn't fail because of poor strategy or execution. You were using broken scientific management principles (short-term thinking, management by objectives) instead of I-Power Continuous Improvement that built American greatness.
narrative_reorganization.coherence_creation: All those management books, MBA theories, consultant advice that didn't deliver sustainable results—they perpetuated scientific management short-termism instead of returning to I-Power principles that fueled American dominance and Japanese miracle.
narrative_reorganization.new_self_concept: Not someone who needs new management techniques, but someone who can reclaim the American business principles that once dominated the world

temporal_analysis.cultural_moment_reference: American business competitiveness crisis, Japanese dominance, 'bad times' economic environment
temporal_analysis.temporal_urgency_device: 'Just when we all need a business miracle' timing

source_file: The Secret of Great Business in Bad Times.md

three_part_structure.what_they_think: Business success requires better strategies, more aggressive management, new theories from business schools, and short-term performance incentives
three_part_structure.what_it_really_is: Business success comes from I-Power (Continuous Improvement)—the American-invented system that emphasizes long-term product excellence, employee empowerment, and continuous innovation over short-term results and top-down control.
three_part_structure.why_nothing_worked: All modern management approaches perpetuate SCIENTIFIC MANAGEMENT and SHORT-TERM THINKING that destroyed American competitiveness. They ignore I-POWER principles that built American greatness and fueled Japanese miracle. Business schools teach wrong system.

root_cause_architecture.villain: Scientific management, business schools, short-term thinking, management by objectives

notable_patterns: American pride/shame dynamic creates emotional urgency
Japanese miracle attribution to American system amplifies 'we forgot our own greatness'
Historical examples from 1890s-1940s establish deep roots
One company result (cut staff in half, tripled sales) is specific proof point
Robert Half endorsement ('best business book ever') from credible source
genius_elements: I-Power naming makes Continuous Improvement sound powerful and actionable
Positioning as 'American invention Japan learned from us' removes 'foreign' objection
Scientific management as villain targets accepted paradigm
Post-WWII shift explanation creates clear before/after narrative
'Quiet revolution' positioning makes reader feel part of movement
Bottom Line/Personal editor credibility (2M+ copies sold) validates author
